Facts.text: Radagaisus, Goth leader who invaded Italy with a large force, captured and executed by the Roman army
Odoacer is proclaimed King of Italy by his troops, becoming the first barbarian King of Italy
King Henry III gives money to Utrecht Deventer diocese
Treaty of Melfi: Pope Nicholas II recognizes the Norman conquest of Southern Italy by appointing Norman adventurer Robert Guiscard Duke of Apulia and Calabria and Count of Sicily
Battle at Kassel: French troops stop uprising of Flemish farmers
Holland and Hanzesteden sign a ceasefire treaty
Battle of Chaldiran ends with a decisive victory for Sultan Selim I of the Ottoman Empire over Shah Ismail I, founder of the Safavid Empire
Rabbi Joseph Caro completes his major work, the "Beit Yosef," a voluminous commentary on the "Arba'ah Turim" code
Bishop Stephen Gardiner is appointed English Lord Chancellor
Beeldenstorm [Iconoclastic Fury] reaches Amsterdam
Francis of Valois, Duke of Anjou, pays tribute to the Earl of Flanders
Michael the Brave confronts the Ottoman army in the Battle of Calugareni
University of Groningen, Netherlands, opens
First one-way streets open in London
Meidingnu Pamheiba is crowned King of Manipur
British fleet under Admiral Hovenden Walker loses 950 men when 8 ships founder on rocks at Île-aux-Oeufs on their way to attack Quebec
Eastern Tennessee settlers declare their area an independent state and name it Franklin; a year later, the Continental Congress rejects it
French Revolution: The National Assembly proclaims freedom of religious opinions
African Methodist Episcopal Church is incorporated
Battle of Großbeeren: Prussians under von Bülow repulse French
Mount Holyoke Female Seminary in South Hadley, Massachusetts, graduates its first class
British capture Hong Kong from China
Skirmish at Big Hill, Kentucky (two Federal regiments)
Union troops and fleet occupy Fort Morgan, Alabama
Treaty of Prague ends the Austro-Prussian War
First carload of rail freight (boots and shoes) arrives in San Francisco from Boston after a 16-day trip
First Japanese commercial ship visits San Francisco carrying tea
Albert Bridge crossing the River Thames in London opens
British Governor-General Charles Gordon of Sudan returns to Cairo
Philadelphia Quakers make 27 errors against Providence Grays in a 28-0 shutout defeat at Messer Street Grounds; wild pitches, walks, and passed balls count as errors in MLB prior to 1888
First ship-to-shore wireless message ("Sherman is sighted") is received in the US from Lightship No. 70 to a coastal receiving station at Cliff House in San Francisco
First Cry of the Philippine Revolution is made in Pugad Lawin, Quezon City, in the province of Manila
National Negro Business League organizes in Boston
Automobile tire chain patented
Chicago White Sox win 19th straight, defeating Washington Senators 4-1 at American League Park
Cuba's first president, Tomás Estrada Palma, asks for US intervention
Pittsburgh Pirates pitcher Howie Camnitz no-hits the NY Giants, 1-0, in a 5-inning game at the Polo Grounds, NYC
Fred Clarke sets a record with four outfield assists for Pittsburgh
Battle of Mons: General Alexander von Kluck's troops force a British withdrawal
General von Hausen executes 612 inhabitants of Dinant, Belgium
German troops plunder Belgium
Japan declares war on Germany in World War I
Tsar Nicolaas II takes control of Russian Army
Military court of Berlin sentences socialist Karl Liebknecht to 4 years
Race riot in Houston, Texas; 2 Black people and 11 white people killed
American swimmer Warren Kealoha wins his first of two consecutive Olympic gold medals in the 100 m backstroke, beating teammate Ray Kegeris at the Antwerp Games
Mary Roberts Rinehart and Avery Hopwood's play "The Bat," based on Rinehart's novel "The Circular Staircase," premieres on Broadway in New York
Austria and the US formally end the war; the US does the same with Germany on the 25th and Hungary on the 29th
British declare a truce with Irish Nationalists Sinn Féin
Capt. Lowell Smith and Lt. John P. Richter perform the first mid-air refueling on a De Havilland DH-4B, setting an endurance flight record of 37 hours
Mars makes its closest approach to Earth since the 10th century
Arab mobs attack Jews in Jerusalem, Mandatory Palestine, triggering a series of violent riots
First British Empire Games close in Hamilton, Canada
Count Gyula Károlyi becomes Prime Minister of Hungary
Philadelphia A's Lefty Grove loses 1-0 to the Browns after winning 16 straight
First televised boxing match, a six-round exhibition at Broadcasting House in London between middleweights Archie Sexton and Laurie Raiteri, airs on BBC TV
England scores 7-903 declared against Australia, with Len Hutton scoring 364
British motorist John Cobb breaks the land speed record, reaching 365.85 mph (589.74 kph) on the Bonneville Salt Flats
German Luftwaffe begins night bombing of London
Battle of Stalingrad: 600 Luftwaffe planes bomb Stalingrad, killing 40,000 people
British Prime MinisterWinston Churchillflies back to London from Cairo, Egypt
First US flights land on Guadalcanal
Last cavalry charge in history takes place during World War II at Isbuschenskij, Russia, when the Italian Savoia Cavalleria charges Soviet infantry
Battle of Kursk, the largest tank battle in history, ends after 50 days as the Soviet Union defeats Germany; over 10,000 tanks take part, and nearly 250,000 combatants are killed
Red Army recaptures Kharkov
94.5°F (34.7°C) in De Bilt, Netherlands, and 101.5°F (38.6°C) in Warnsveld
Allied troops capture Marseille, France
Drancy internment camp in Drancy is liberated
Romanian Royal Coup: King Michael orders forces to cease fire against Allies and dismisses pro-Axis Prime Minister, Marshal Ion Antonescu
US 20th Army Corps enters Fontainebleau and Melun de Seine
US B-24 crashes into a school in Freckleton, England, killing 76
13th NFL Chicago All-Star Game: All-Stars 16, Los Angeles 0 (97,380 attendees)
Ordinance No. 46 of the British Military Government constitutes the German state of Schleswig-Holstein
US President Truman's daughter, Margaret, gives her first public singing concert
Swedish diplomat Folke Bernadotte of the United Nations asks for aid for fugitives to Palestine
World Council of Churches is formed by 147 churches from 44 countries
West Germany and Japan are readmitted to the International Amateur Athletic Federation
Arab League security pact goes into effect
Giants' Bob Elliott is ejected for arguing a strike, Bobby Hoffman completes his at-bat, strikes out, and is also ejected for arguing
Kitty Wells becomes the first woman to reach #1 on the Billboard Country chart with "It Wasn't God Who Made Honky Tonk Angels" and stays at the top for 15 weeks
Dutch cyclist Arie Van Vliet becomes world champion sprinter
Dutch DC-6 crashes near IJmuiden in the North Sea, killing 21
Former Boston Braves pitcher Phil Paine becomes the first major leaguer to play in Japan while on military service with the U.S. Air Force, playing the first of nine games for the Nishitetsu Lions
KBAK TV channel 29 in Bakersfield, CA (ABC) begins broadcasting
Phil Grate sets a record for throwing a baseball 443 ft 3 in (135.1 m)
USSR performs a nuclear test
First flight of the C-130 Hercules transport aircraft
US performs a nuclear test at the Nevada Test Site
"Musical Marie" Ashton completes a 133-hour piano marathon at the Plaza-Central cinema in England, setting a female record for continuous piano playing
People's Republic of China resumes bombardment of the Quemoy and Matsu islands in the Second Taiwan Strait Crisis
World's largest frog, a Goliath frog weighing 3.3 kg, is found in Equatorial Guinea
Belgium sends troops to Rwanda-Urundi
East Germany imposes new curbs on travel between West and East Berlin
Ranger 1, a US test flight, fails to reach its intended high Earth orbit and re-enters and burns up on August 30, 1961
First Europe-US live TV program via Telstar
Beatles release single "She Loves You" in the UK
US performs a nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Lunar Orbiter 1 takes the first photograph of Earth from the Moon
The Beatles' last New York City concert takes place in Queens, at Shea Stadium before a crowd of 45,000 with 11,000 unsold seats
Ringo temporarily quits The Beatles over a disagreement and rejoins the group after a holiday in Sardinia
Yankees and Tigers play a 3-3 tie in 19 innings due to a 1 am curfew
American Audrey McElmory wins the World Cycling Championships in Brno, Czechoslovakia
France's Une De Mai wins the International Trot at Roosevelt Raceway, becoming the first non-American since 1912 to win a cycling race title
Roberto Clementecompiles his record second consecutive 5-hit game
WGTU TV channel 29 in Traverse City, MI (ABC) begins broadcasting
Four civilians and one British soldier are injured in separate overnight shooting incidents in Northern Ireland

MLB Chicago White Sox slugger Dick Allen becomes the fourth player to homer into Comiskey Park's center field bleachers, a feat previously achieved by only Jimmie Foxx,Hank Greenberg, and Alex Johnson
Intelsat communications satellite is launched
Norrmalmstorg robbery and six-day siege begin in Stockholm, the first Swedish crime filmed live on TV and the origin of the term Stockholm Syndrome
British "Free" guitarist Paul Kossoff is revived after a heart attack but dies in 1976
Classical Way wins the Championship Cup at Roosevelt Raceway
Communists take over Laos
Ethiopian junta under Mengistu Haile Mariam
Philip Kapleau conducts the first jukai ceremony in Poland
USSR performs a nuclear test at Novaya Zemlya, USSR
A 6.4-magnitude earthquake strikes China, killing thousands
First human-powered flight over a mile by Bryan Allen in the Gossamer Condor, designed by Paul MacCready, wins the first Kremer Prize
Marxist philosopher Rudolf Bahro is imprisoned in East Germany
Iranian students occupy the Iranian embassy in Wassenaar
Iranian army opens offensive against Kurds
United Nations' Vienna office opens
Charles O. Finley sells the Oakland A's MLB franchise for $12.7 million to Walter Haas Jr., owner and CEO of Levi Strauss
Lebanese Phalangist leader Bachir Gemayel is elected as president
Seattle Mariners pitcherGaylord Perryis ejected for throwing a spitter
USSR performs a nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Paul Hornungis awarded $1,160,000 by a Louisville court against the NCAA, which barred him as a college football analyst for betting on games
Said Aouita of Morocco sets a 1,500-meter record (3:29.46) in Berlin
South African attorney and UDF leader Dullah Omar is arrested
West German top counter-espionage agent Hans Tiedge moves to East Germany
15-year-old boy hijacks KLM B737 and demands $1 billion
Heavy rainfall and floods in Bangladesh kill hundreds of people
Lewis, Everett, Burrell, and Heard run a world record 4 x 200 m (1:19.38)
The LA Dodgers beat the Montreal Expos 1-0 in 22 innings with a home run by Rick Dempsey
Two million people form a human chain across Latvia, Estonia, and Lithuania in a peaceful pro-independence demonstration against Soviet occupation
Armenia declares independence from the Soviet Union
East and West Germany announce they will reunite on October 3
US begins call up of 46,000 reservists to the Persian Gulf
Dennis Eckersley, who previously set the record for most consecutive saves (40), is the first pitcher to record 40 saves in four different seasons
Wilhelm Verwoerd, grandson of former South African Prime Minister Hendrik Verwoerd, the main architect of apartheid, joins the African National Congress
Dow Jones Industrial Average reaches record high of 3,638.96 points
Fred McGriff andDavid Justiceare the sixth to hit back-to-back home runs twice in the same game
ActorLarry Hagmanreceives a liver transplant
Indians' José Mesa sets a record with his 38th consecutive save
A Gulf Air Airbus A320 crashes into the Persian Gulf near Manama, Bahrain, killing 143 people
Nicaragua becomes a member of the Berne Convention copyright treaty, essentially deprecating the Buenos Aires Convention treaty because all members of the BA Convention are also signatories to Berne
An American sweep in the 400 m medals at the Athens Olympics; Jeremy Wariner wins gold in 44.00 ahead of Otis Harris and Derrick Brew
Outstanding British athlete Kelly Holmes wins the first leg of her 800/1,500m double at the Athens Olympics, taking the gold medal in 1:56.38
Winning pitcher Lisa Fernandez (4-0) leads the US to the softball gold medal at the Athens Olympics, beating Australia 5-1
TANS Peru Flight 204 crashes near Pucallpa, Peru, killing 41
Natascha Kampusch, abducted at the age of 10, manages to escape from her captor Wolfgang Priklopil after eight years of captivity
The #hashtag is invented and first used in a tweet by US product designer Chris Messina

Australian diver Matthew Mitcham wins the men's 10 m platform gold medal with a final dive at the Beijing Olympics, preventing the Chinese from winning every diving event and achieving the highest score for an individual dive in Olympic history (112.10)
Midfielder Ángel Di María scores a 58th-minute winner as Argentina beats Nigeria 1-0 to win the men's football gold medal at the Beijing Olympics; 89,102 attend at the National Stadium
The Tradition Senior Men's Golf, Crosswater Club: Mike Reid wins his second Champions Tour major with birdie on first playoff hole against John Cook
A hostage crisis occurs at the Quirino Grandstand in Manila, where a dismissed police officer takes hostage a tour bus full of Chinese nationals
A 5.8 earthquake occurs in Mineral, Virginia, and is felt as far north as Ontario and as far south as Atlanta, Georgia
At least 30 people are killed as a result of monsoon rain in Rajasthan, India
Four people are killed and 28 injured in a hot air balloon accident in Slovenia
26 people are killed and 55 are injured by a suicide bombing in Baghdad, Iraq
Fifty people are killed in mosque bombings in Tripoli, Lebanon
UN inspectors are stopped by the Syrian government from investigating a reported site of a chemical massacre
Alex Puccioplaces second in the Boulder World Championship in Munich, Germany
A 12-year-old boy trips and rips the 17th-century painting "Flowers" by Paolo Porpora, worth $1.5 million, at an exhibition in Taiwan
Destruction by ISIS of the first-century AD Temple of Baalshamin in the ancient ruins of Palmyra is confirmed by Syrian officials
English IndyCar driver Justin Wilson suffers a head injury during a race crash at Pocono Raceway in Long Pond, Pennsylvania
Airstrike on hotel in Yemen's capital, Sanaa, by Saudi-led coalition leaves at least 30 dead
India's toll from swine flu rises above 1,000 for the year, with 22,186 cases reported
Nearly 60 million people in the Indus Valley, Pakistan, are at risk from arsenic, according to research published in "Science Advances"
US Navy fires Vice Admiral Joseph Aucoin as commander of the Seventh Fleet following a series of collisions in Asian waters
World's driest place, the Atacama Desert in Chile, blooms after unexpected rainfall
Russia launches the first floating nuclear power station, the Akademik Lomonosov, from the port of Murmansk
Kellyanne Conway, adviser to President Trump, announces she is stepping away from the White House for family reasons
US Food and Drug Administration grants full approval to Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 vaccine for people 16 and over, the first vaccine to move past emergency-use status in the US
Video evidence of a Seychelles giant tortoise hunting and eating a bird is revealed for the first time; tortoises were previously thought to be herbivores
In a US domestic terrorism case, two men are found guilty of plotting to kidnap Michigan Governor Gretchen Whitmer
At least 26 people are killed after a railway bridge under construction collapses near the Indian town of Sairang in Mizoram state
Greek wildfires claim 20 lives, including 18 migrants trying to cross the border from Turkey
India's Chandrayaan-3 lunar mission is the first to land at the Moon's southern pole successfully and makes India the fourth country to ever land on the Moon

Shohei Ohtaniof the Los Angeles Angels steals a base and hits a grand slam in a 7-3 win at home over Tampa Bay, becoming just the sixth MLB player to have 40 home runs and 40 stolen bases in a season; he does so in the fewest number of games, 126
US independence presidential candidate Robert F. Kennedy Jr.  suspends his campaign and endorsesDonald Trump
Battle at Florence:Stilicho's Roman army beats Gothic leader Radagaisus' Barbarian force of possibly 200,000, drafting some into the Roman army and selling the rest into slavery
Governor Francisco de Bobadilla arrives in the Indies and soon after arrests and sends former GovernorChristopher Columbusback to Spain in chains
French explorerJacques Cartierlands near Quebec City on his third voyage to Canada accompanied by five ships and 1500 people to establish a colony (colony fails after two years)
French Revolution: The National Convention adopts the levée en masse, conscripting all able-bodied men between 18 and 25 for military service during the French Revolutionary Wars
Napoleonleaves Egypt for France en route to seizing power
Sixth Zionist Congress:Theodor Herzldeclares a Jewish state
British Prime MinisterH. H. Asquithholds secret meeting about British strategy in case of war with Germany
Paavo Nurmiof Finland runs a world record for 1 mile with a time of 4:10.4 in Stockholm; the record stands until 1931
Mahatma Gandhireleased from Indian jail following another hunger strike
17-year-old Cleveland Indians future Baseball Hall of Fame pitcherBob Fellerplays his first MLB game as a starter and strikes out 15 St. Louis Browns in a 4-1 win at League Park, Cleveland
"You Can't Take It With You" from the play by George S. Kaufman andMoss Hart, directed byFrank Capraand starringJames Stewartand Jean Arthur, premieres and wins Best Picture in 1939
Nazi Germany and the Soviet Union agree to theMolotov-Ribbentropnon-aggression pact and secretly divide Poland between themselves, setting the stage for World War II
QueenWilhelmina of the Netherlandsdismisses Dutch Prime Minister Dirk Jan de Geer
Walter Johnsonpitches toBabe Ruthin a pregame attraction that draws 69,000 for the NYY-Washington MLB doubleheader at Yankee Stadium and raises $80,000 for Army-Navy relief
British generalBernard Montgomeryconsults with US GeneralsOmar BradleyandDwight D. Eisenhower
Free FrenchGeneral Leclerc's 2nd Armored Division advances towards Paris
Premiere of "The Big Sleep," directed by Howard Hawks and starringHumphrey Bogartas Philip Marlowe andLauren Bacall
Italian Ferrari driverAlberto Ascariwins the Swiss Grand Prix at Bremgarten, clinching his second Formula 1 World Drivers' Championship
PresidentGetúlio Vargasof Brazil resigns temporarily
DrummerRingo Starrreveals he wrote a song titled "Don't Pass Me By" in an interview, the Beatles don' t record it until 1968
Republican convention in Miami Beach, Florida, renominates Vice PresidentSpiro Agnew, but not unanimously as one vote goes to NBC newsmanDavid Brinkley
John Lennonreports seeing a UFO in New York City
Mike Tysonand Mitch Green brawl at 4 a.m. in Harlem, NYC
Jeff Buckley releases his album "Grace," featuring his cover ofLeonard Cohen's "Hallelujah"
Osama bin Ladenissues a message entitled "A Declaration of War Against the Americans Occupying the Land of the Two Holy Places"

San Francisco Giants sluggerBarry Bondsbecomes the first MLB player to hit 400 home runs and steal 400 bases in a career when he homers off Florida Marlins' Kirt Ojala at Pro Player Stadium in Miami Gardens, Florida
Ethiopian distance runnerKenenisa Bekelewraps up the 5,000/10,000m double at the Beijing Olympics when he takes gold in the 5,000m in an Olympic record 12:57.82
The star-studded American women's basketball team, led by Lisa Leslie, Sue Bird, andDiana Taurasi, wins the gold medal at the Beijing Olympics with a convincing 92-65 victory over Australia
Libyan leaderMuammar al-Gaddafiis overthrown after National Transitional Council forces take control of the Bab al-Azizia compound during the 2011 Libyan Civil War
TenorPlácido Domingosings his 150th opera role inBizet’s “The Pearl Fishers” at the Salzburg Festival, Austria
American Black manJacob Blakeis shot and injured by police in front of his children in Kenosha, Wisconsin, prompting violent protests
US Republican Party convention begins by formally renominatingDonald Trumpfor a second presidential term
Former Malaysian Prime MinisterNajib Razakbegins a 12-year prison sentence for money laundering after the High Court rejects his final appeal
Nicki Minaj's single "Super Freaky Girl" debuts at #1, the first female rapper to do so since Lauryn Hill in 1998 with "Doo Wop (That Thing)"
Yevgeny Prigozhin, head of the Wagner mercenary group, is reported killed along with nine others in a plane crash northwest of Moscow, according to the Russian Federal Air Transport Agency
Mount Vesuvius begins stirring on the feast day of Vulcan, the Roman god of fire, and goes on to destroy Pompeii [approximate date]
Scottish patriotWilliam Wallaceis executed for high treason by Edward I of England at Smithfield, London
Hurricane Katrina forms over the Bahamas and later becomes a Category 5 hurricane
Charles Martel, Frankish leader and general (Battle of Tours), grandfather of Charlemagne, born in Herstal, Belgium (d. 741)
Siegmund Freiherr von Herberstein, Austrian diplomat, born in Vipava, Slovenia (d. 1566)
Francois Hotman/Hotomanus, French lawyer and diplomat (Anti-Tribonien), born in Paris (d. 1590)
Fulvio Testi, Italian poet (Pianto d'Italia), born in Ferrara, Italy (d. 1646)
Stanisław Lubieniecki, Polish astronomer, born in Raków, Kielce County, Poland (d. 1675)
Abraham Yates, American Continental Congressman, born in Albany, New York (d. 1796)
Friedrich Hartmann Graf, German composer, born in Rudolstadt, Schwarzburg-Rudolstadt, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1795)
Ivan VI, Emperor of Russia (1740-41), born in Saint Petersburg, Russia (d. 1764)
Jean-François de Galaup, comte de La Pérouse, French explorer (led 1785 voyage of discovery), born in Albi, France (d. c. 1788?)
Louis-François Beffara, French lexicographer, born in Nonancourt, France (d. 1838)
Jakob F. Fries, German philosopher, born in Barby, Germany (d. 1843)
William Tierney Clark, English civil engineer, born in Bristol, England (d. 1852)
Anton von Schmerling, Austrian statesman, born in Lichtental, Vienna, Austria (d. 1893)
James Roosevelt Bayley, first Bishop of Newark, New Jersey, and the eighth Archbishop of Baltimore, born in New York City (d. 1877)
Rufus Ingalls, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Denmark, Maine (d. 1893)
Moritz B. Cantor, German historian of mathematics, born in Mannheim, Duchy of Baden, German Confederation (d. 1920)
Alexander Chambers, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Cattaraugus, New York (d. 1888)
Marie Henriette of Austria, Queen of the Belgians, born in Buda Castle, Budapest, Hungary (d. 1902)
William Southam, Canadian newspaper publisher, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1932)
Alexander Milne Calder, Scottish-American sculptor, born in Aberdeen, Scotland (d. 1923)
Sarah Frances Whiting, American physicist and astronomer, born in Wyoming, New York (d. 1927)
Alois Jirásek, Czech novelist (The White Mountain), magazine editor (Zorn), and playwright, born in Hronov, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1930)
Arnold Toynbee, English economic historian and social reformer, born in London (d. 1883)
Clímaco Calderón, President of Colombia for one day in 1882, born in Santa Rosa de Viterbo, Colombia (d. 1913)
Moritz Moszkowski, Polish-German composer, born in Breslau, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1925)
Eleftherios K. Venizalos, Premier of Greece (1910-15, 28-32), born in Mournies, Greece (d. 1936)
Marcel Schwob, French writer and journalist (Le Croisade des Enfants), born in Chaville, France (d. 1905)
Paul Otlet, Belgian author, lawyer and peace activist (created the Universal Decimal Classification scheme), born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1944)
Edgar Lee Masters, American poet and novelist (Spoon River Anthology), born in Garnett, Kansas (d. 1950)
James (Sunny Jim) Rolph, San Francisco mayor (1912-31), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1934)
George Davis, American Baseball HOF shortstop and manager (New York Giants; World Series 1906 Chicago WS), born in Cohoes, New York (d. 1940)
Eugene Lanceray, Russian artist, born in Pavlovsk, Saint Petersburg, Russia (d. 1946)
William Eccles, English physicist and radio pioneer, born in Barrow-in-Furness, England (d. 1966)
Alfrēds Kalniņš, Latvian organist, composer (Baņuta), conductor, and pedagogue, born in Cēsis, Russian Empire (d. 1951)
Alexander Grin, Russian novelist (Jessie and Morgiana), born in Vyatka, Kirov Oblast, Russia (d. 1932)
Louis Schelfhout, Dutch painter and graphic artist, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1943)
Jonathan Mayhew Wainwright IV, American army officer, born in Fort Walla Walla, Washington (d. 1953)
Ogden L. Mills, U.S. Secretary of the Treasury (1927-1932), born in Newport, Rhode Island (d. 1937)
Will Cuppy, American humorist (The Decline and Fall of Practically Everybody), born in Auburn, Indiana (d. 1949)
Henry Thomas Tizard, English chemist and inventor, born in Gillingham, Kent, England (d. 1959)
Gottfried Rudinger, German composer, born in Lindau, Kingdom of Bavaria (d. 1946)
Jo Sternheim, Dutch actor and playwright (Fatherland), born in Amsterdam (d. 1956)
Alfred Lichtenstein, German writer, born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1914)
Valdemar Eiberg, Danish jazz pioneer, saxophonist, and bandleader ("I've Got a Cross-Eyed Papa"), born in Kolding, Denmark (d. 1965)
Roy Agnew, Australian composer (Sonata Ballade), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1944)
Gareth Hughes, Welsh actor (Midnight Girl), born in Dafen, Carmarthenshire, Wales (d. 1965)
James La Guma, South African political activist and trade unionist, born in Cape Town (d. 1961)
Ernst Krenek, Austrian-American concert, opera, and electronic music composer (Johnny Spielt Auf: Orpheus and Eurydike; Fibonacci-Mobile), born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 1991)
Malvina Reynolds, American folk singer and songwriter (Morningtown Ride), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1978)
Cecil Rolph Hewitt, English journalist and policeman, born in London (d. 1994)
John Sherman Cooper, American jurist, politician (US Senator), and diplomat (Ambassador to India, 1954-55;  Ambassador to East Germany, 1974-75), born in Somerset, Kentucky (d. 1991)
Mauritius van Haegendoren, Flemish historian and senator, born in Leuven, Belgium (d. 1994)
William Primrose, Scottish violist (Method for Violin & Viola), born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1982)
(John) "Rags" Ragland, American burlesque comedian, and stage and screen character actor (Whistling In The Dark), born in Louisville, Kentucky (d. 1946)
(Leonard) Constant Lambert, British composer (The Rio Grande; Horoscope; Summer's Last Will and Testament), and music director (Vic-Wells/Royal Ballet, 1931-47), born in Fulham, London (d. 1951)
Arthur Adamov, Russian-French playwright (Paolo Paoli), born in Kislovodsk, Terek Oblast, Russia (d. 1970)
Hannah Frank, Scottish artist and sculptor, born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 2008)
Syd Buller, English cricket umpire (33 Tests 1956-69), born in Leeds, England (d. 1970)
Giuseppe Meazza, Italian footballer, born in Milan, Italy (d. 1979)
Albert Alberts, Dutch writer and journalist (French battle), born in Haarlem, Netherlands (d. 1996)
Birger Ruud, Norwegian ski jumper (Olympic gold 1932, 36; World C'ship gold 1931, 35, 37), born in Kongsberg, Norway (d. 1998)
Carl Dolmetsch, French music director (Haslemere Festival), born in Fontenay-sous-Bois, France (d. 1997)
Elizabeth "Betty" Robinson, US 100m sprinter (Olympic gold 1928), born in Riverdale, Illinois (d. 1999)
Harry Kissin, Baron Kissin, British businessman and President (GPG), born in Gdańsk, Poland (d. 1997)
Jara Ribnikar, Serbian writer, born in Königgrätz, Bohemia (d. 2007)
Bob Crosby, American jazz singer and bandleader (Bob-Cats), born in Spokane, Washington (d. 1993)
Luis Felipe Ramón y Rivera, Venezuelan musician and composer, born in San Cristóbal, Táchira, Venezuela (d. 1993)
Tex Williams, American country-western singer, born in Ramsey, Illinois (d. 1985)
Robert Crichton-Brown, Australian businessman and CEO (Rothmans International), born in Melbourne, Australia (d. 2013)
Aart Verstegen, Dutch ballet dancer and choreographer (Jolly Joker's Last Joke), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1998)
Kenneth Arrow, American economist, (Nobel Prize, 1972), born in New York City (d. 2017)
Sam Cook, English cricket spin bowler in (1 Test; Gloucestershire CCC), born in Tetbury, England (d. 1996)
Brian Young, Chairman (Christian Aid), born in Sri Lanka (d. 2016)
Eevi Huttunen, Finnish speed skater (8 World C'ship gold 1951-59), born in Karttula, Finland (d. 2015)
George Kell, American Baseball HOF third baseman (10 x MLB All-Star; AL batting champion 1949; Detroit Tigers, Boston Red Sox), born in Swifton, Arkansas (d. 2009)
Jack Boddy, British trade union leader, born in Norwich (d. 2004)
Jean Darling [Dorothy Jean LeVake], American child actress (Our Gang), born in Santa Monica, California (d. 2015)
Pierre Gauvreau, Canadian painter, television writer and producer, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2011)
Humphrey Fisher, TV producer, born in England (d. 1996)
Wynona Carr, American gospel, R&B and rock and roll singer-songwriter ("The Ball Game", "Should I Ever Love Again?"), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 1976)[1]
Denis Sargan, British econometrician, born in Doncaster, Yorkshire (d. 1996)
Edvard Fliflet Braein, Norwegian composer (Ut mot havet (Out To Sea); Anne Pedersdotter), born in Kristiansund, Norway (d. 1976)
Ephraim Kishon, Israeli author, dramatist, screenwriter, and Oscar-nominated film director, born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 2005)
Siti Hartinah, Second First Lady of Indonesia, wife of President Suharto of Indonesia, born in Jaten village, Surakarta, Central Java (d. 1996)
Sulkhan Tsintsadze, Georgian composer, born in Gori, Transcaucasian SSR, Soviet Union (d. 1991)
Włodzimierz Kotoński, Polish composer, born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 2014)
Clifford Geertz, American anthropologist (symbolic anthropology), born in San Francisco, California (d. 2006)
Dick Bruna, Dutch illustrator (Miffy books), born in Utrecht (d. 2017)
Houari Boumédiènne, President of Algeria (1965-78), born in Clauzel, Algeria (d. 1978)
John Hoskyns, English Policy Advisor to Margaret Thatcher (1979-82), born in Farnborough, England (d. 2014)
Martial Solal, French jazz pianist and composer, born in Algiers, French Algeria (d. 2024)[1]
Peter Wyngarde [Cyril Goldbert], British actor (Department S and Jason King), born in Marseille, France (d. 2018)
